channel.title: Radio National Breakfast - Breakfast Wrap
channel.description: Filtered feed containing only Breakfast Wrap episodes from Radio National Breakfast
channel.itunes:author: ABC Radio National

item.title: Breakfast Wrap: Reuters chief on the future of news
item.description:
As artificial intelligence swarms all aspects of our lives, we'll hear how the boss of one of the world's biggest media companies is managing the transformation. 

Then Victoria is introducing new laws to un-mask anonymous trolls who vilify others online, especially minors. 

And we get an immediate reaction to the World Cup final from Australia's own Craig Foster, and we'll hear how US President Donald Trump still managed to steal the spotlight from the Spanish mens side.

Recap the morning's news, politics and global affairs with the Breakfast Wrap.

item.title: Breakfast Wrap: Smoking rate data exposes illicit tobacco challenge
item.description:
We'll unpack new data showing the challenge ahead on the illicit tobacco trade, and dive into extraordinary comments made by Senator Pauline Hanson on Tommy Robinson's podcast. 

And we'll cap the week marking 60 years of Play School with one of the show's most iconic presenters Benita Collings. 

Recap the morning's news, politics and global affairs with the Breakfast Wrap.

item.title: Breakfast Wrap: Hanson's controversial "fact-finding" mission with Britain's far-right
item.description: Coverage and analysis of national and international events.

item.title: Breakfast Wrap: Inside big AI
item.description:
As the Prime Minister unveils a new national framework on artificial intelligence, we take you inside one of the world's biggest AI companies with a former board member of Open AI.

Then, a string of alleged domestic violence tragedies is prompting new questions on what could really make a difference to an epidemic of violence against women and children.

And the Royal Commission into Antisemitism and Social Cohesion continues its focus on universities today - so we hear from Bill Shorten, the Vice Chancellor of the University of Canberra.

Recap the morning's news, politics and global affairs with the Breakfast Wrap.

item.title: Breakfast Wrap: "Gobsmacking" US plans for Strait of Hormuz
item.description:
The battle for control of the Strait of Hormuz intensified overnight, with Washington saying it will immediately reinstate its blockade of Iran ports - and impose a 20 per cent charge on all cargo shipped.

A former US Ambassador to the United Arab Emirates shares their take on the President's plans. 

Meanwhile a new report from the eSafety Commission has revealed significant gaps in the way major tech companies are tackling child sexual exploitation and abuse.

Then, we'll hear from the university sector with new requirements coming in for institutions to adopt definitions on antisemitism, Islamophobia and racism.

And we'll hear a tribute to the late actor and legendary Kiwi Sam Neill. 

Recap the morning's news, politics and global affairs with the Breakfast Wrap.

item.title: Breakfast Wrap: Universities required to adopt definitions of antisemitism and Islamophobia
item.description:
Australia universities will be required to adopt formal definitions of antisemitism, Islamophobia and racism against Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander peoples, under a new standard starting from today.

We hear from the Home Affairs Minister Tony Burke, and the Shadow Education Minister Julian Leeser. 

Then, there's an unwelcome addition to the Aussie music charts - a song created with AI-assisted technology. 

The local music industry is campaigning for the government to maintain copyright laws amid lobbying from AI companies to do away certain protections. 

Recap the morning's news, politics and global affairs with the Breakfast Wrap.

item.title: Breakfast Wrap: Modi comes to Melbourne
item.description:
We take a closer look at the Indian Prime Minister Narendra Modi's trip to Melbourne. 

The two day leaders summit forged agreements to deepen defence ties, share more military information, also to finalise a uranium export deal.

And it culminated in a massive event at Melbourne's Docklands stadium attended by thousands of supporters and community members. 

We'll hear from those attending, and go behind the spectacle. 

We'll speak to the Greens, the Resources Minister, Australia's former Ambassador to India, also Human Rights Watch and Indian diaspora media covering this week's meetings. 

Recap the morning's news, politics and global affairs with the Breakfast Wrap.

item.title: Breakfast Wrap: Telstra under fire
item.description:
Telstra says network faults persist today - while it continues to conduct welfare checks on customers who failed to access Triple Zero.

We hear from the Federal Government, the Opposition, and the Telecommunications Industry Ombudsman as Telstra cleans up. 

Then, India's Prime Minister Narendra Modi will meet Australian PM Anthony Albanese today, where they're expected to secure a landmark uranium agreement - and boost defence ties.

Recap the morning's news, politics and global affairs with the Breakfast Wrap.

item.title: Breakfast Wrap: Scandals engulf Victorian political parties
item.description:
With a state election less than 150 days away, Victoria's two major parties are engulfed in scandal. 

The Labor Premier has been referred to the anti-corruption watchdog over the state's big build scandal.

And the Liberal party is in disarray as one MP launches legal action right as the party was set to disendorse her.

We hear from the Shadow Attorney General James Newbury.

Then,  two far-right leaders in Europe are vowing to fight what will be significant elections - we'll find out what's going on with Marine le Pen and Nigel Farage.

And we'll take a look at the Prime Minister's diplomacy in the Pacific this week. 

Recap the morning's news, politics and global affairs with the Breakfast Wrap.

item.title: Breakfast Wrap: Trump's red card call stirs World Cup storm
item.description: Coverage and analysis of national and international events.

item.title: Breakfast Wrap: The Prime Minister's Pacific Diplomacy Blitz
item.description: Coverage and analysis of national and international events.

item.title: Breakfast Wrap: David Pocock on Labor's gambling ad reforms
item.description:
Crossbenchers have helped force Senate committee inquiries into the contentious gambling regulation and social media bills.

Then, will the government's changes to the automated assessment process for in-home care funding improve the situation for older Australians? 

And the federal government has pledged $2 million in humanitarian aid to support communities affected by last week's twin earthquakes.

Recap the morning's news, politics and global affairs with the Breakfast Wrap.

item.title: Breakfast Wrap: Who's getting help in Venezuela, and who isn't
item.description:
There's growing outrage at the Venezuelan government's response to the twin earthquakes, with many Venezuelans accusing authorities of abandoning them for days without any equipment or personnel - leaving them to search for survivors with their bare hands.

We hear from a journalist on the ground speaking to affected families. 

Then, the government gets into discussions with the Opposition and the crossbench over its gambling ad restriction legislation. 

And we return to the Aged Care Minister for an update on a controversial algorithm-assisted tool.

Recap the morning's news, politics and global affairs with the Breakfast Wrap.

item.title: Breakfast Wrap: Gambling ad restrictions set to hit parliament
item.description:
The government's gambling reforms are expected to go before parliament tomorrow amid fierce opposition from the Greens and cross-bench MPs who say the restrictions on advertising don't go nearly far enough. 

We'll hear from the Greens and the federal opposition. 

Then, there are major changes facing youth digital media, and some of the biggest mainstream companies, as the cost of business rises and AI processes are adopted across the industry. We'll hear from the former co-founder of Junkee media ahead of their National Press Club address. 

And Carrington Clarke will fill us in on all the new financial year updates you should be across. 

Recap the morning's news, politics and global affairs with the Breakfast Wrap.

item.title: Breakfast Wrap: What is the Nakamal agreement?
item.description:
After months of stalled negotiations, Australia has inked a landmark strategic pact with the Pacific Island nation of Vanuatu.

So what does it mean for both parties?

And we look at chaos in South Africa, as protest groups demand that all undocumented foreigners leave the country by today, ahead of anti-immigration rallies that many fear will turn violent.

Recap the morning's news, politics and global affairs with the Breakfast Wrap.

item.title: Breakfast Wrap: Victoria's Big Build woes
item.description:
Nine newspapers are reporting that a two-year attempted clean-up of Victoria's big build has failed to stop sums of money flowing from state and federally funded projects through to the underworld. 

We hear from the federal opposition and government on the revelations from 60 Minutes. 

Then, independent MP Monique Ryan explains why she wants to change the date of HECS indexation, and what she makes of the new party formed by two high profile independents. 

And we turn to the crisis in Venezuela, following the devastating twin earthquakes last week. 

Recap the morning's news, politics and global affairs with the Breakfast Wrap.

item.title: Breakfast Wrap: Western Sydney Special
item.description: Agenda-setting news coverage, breaking news and stories impacting the lives of all Australians.

item.title: Breakfast Wrap: ASIO warns of 'unprecedented threats' facing Australlia
item.description: Coverage and analysis of national and international events.

item.title: Breakfast Wrap: Is there a correction going on in Australia's housing market?
item.description: Coverage and analysis of national and international events.

item.title: Breakfast Wrap: Starmer out with Burnham bound for Number 10
item.description:
Sir Keir Starmer has become the sixth British prime minister to leave office in the past 10 years.

It paves the way for newly-elected MP Andy Burnham to vie for the top job, following last week's by-election victory. 

We get the views from across Westminster on a monumental day in British political history. 

From the journalists covering the developments at Downing street, then inside Keir Starmers camp, with his former political director, and one of the backbenchers who's stood by his side. 

We also hear from a Conservative peer in the House of Lords, who's also handily a polling analyst.

Recap the morning's news, politics and global affairs with the Breakfast Wrap.

item.title: Breakfast Wrap: H5 bird flu detected in Australia for first time
item.description:
The deadly H5N1 bird flu has officially been detected for the first time on mainland Australia.

The highly contagious variant - which has now reached every continent - has devastated wildlife populations overseas.

We hear from Australia's Chief Veterinary Office.

Then, Labor Senator Deborah O'Neill discusses last week's a fiery Senate inquiry testimony from former KPMG boss Andrew Yates. 

And we look at the political uncertainty facing the future of Keir Starmer's Prime Ministership. 

Recap the morning's news, politics and global affairs with the Breakfast Wrap.

item.title: Breakfast Wrap: Treasurer defends backdown over CGT carve-outs
item.description:
The federal government has unveiled carve-outs to its proposed capital gains tax changes, following complaints from business groups and start-ups.

A 50% capital gains concession for small businesses will be expanded to all those with a turnover up to 10 million dollars, and there will also be carve outs for startups and discretionary testamentary trusts. So how is the federal Treasurer defending the change?

Recap the morning's news, politics and global affairs with the Breakfast Wrap.

item.title: Breakfast Wrap: Hanson's speech slammed by critics
item.description:
Pauline Hanson gave one of the longest speeches heard at the National Press Club in Canberra yesterday — and certainly one of the most controversial.

The One Nation leader — riding high in recent polling — advocated for a "mono-cultural" society by restricting immigration, targeting what she called "radical Islam".

Today we hear from the Special Envoy to Combat Islamophobia, and from the President of the Human Rights Commission. The new One Nation MP for Farrer also shares his views, and Tom Crowley explains how the speech will be used politically by all sides. 

Recap the morning's news, politics and global affairs with the Breakfast Wrap.

item.title: Breakfast Wrap: Australia looks into sexual assault claims against Israeli forces
item.description:
The AFP is probing allegations that Israeli forces sexually assaulted Australian activists involved in a humanitarian flotilla last month, accusations denied by the Israeli Ambassador to Australia.

On the Breakfast Wrap you'll hear from a father of one of the activists making these accusations, who attended a meeting with the AFP and Foreign Minister on the allegations. And we'll put his comments to the Israeli Ambassador to Australia Hillel Newman. 

Then, comments from the Shadow Treasurer Tim Wilson are drawing criticism from Labor. He made the comments during an interview today on Radio National Breakfast, so what has he actually said?

Recap the morning's news, politics and global affairs with the Breakfast Wrap

item.title: Breakfast Wrap: How Israel could derail Trump's Iran deal
item.description:
The world is waiting for further details on the deal signed between the US and Iran to stop the war in its tracks, but why could Israel's continuing invasion of Lebanon derail the prospects for peace?

We'll hear from the former US Ambassador to Lebanon and find out what the Australian government's response is. 

Then, the UK is following in Australia's footsteps with their own teen social media ban, so how is Britain trying to go even further?

And you'll hear an exclusive interview with one of the crew of NASA's next Artemis mission. 

Recap the morning's news, politics and global affairs with the Breakfast Wrap.

item.title: Breakfast Wrap: Peace agreement signed between Iran and US
item.description:
US President Donald Trump says the United States and Iran have agreed to a ceasefire deal to end their war.While all the terms of the deal are yet to be announced, we know the Strait of Hormuz is set to be reopened.

We also hear about the President's UFC match on the White House lawn. 

We explore an attempt to limit Switzerland's population to 10 million people.

And Socceroos legends react to a historic opening match at the World Cup. 

Recap the morning's news, politics and global affairs with the Breakfast Wrap.
